messages.content:
AUDIO TRANSCRIPT
 

Time	
Podcast Episode Highlights
0:36	Intro. [Recording date: October 29, 2009.] Quote from book:
Private ownership usually creates wealth, but too much ownership has the opposite effect. It creates gridlock. Gridlock is a free-market paradox, when too many people own too many pieces of one thing, cooperation breaks down, wealth disappears, and everybody loses.
Explain. Gridlock--too many owners--riff on familiar concept of tragedy of the commons. In a tragedy of the commons, too few owners, resulting in overuse of the resource. Too few owners in the ocean and people over-fish it; too few owners of the air, and people pollute it. Solution to tragedy of the commons is to privatize--single decision-maker who invests in the lake so there are more fish tomorrow. But privatization can overshoot. Instead of too few owners and a tragedy of the commons, we can have too many owners, and a tragedy of the anticommons. Instead of a resource being wasted in the economic sense through overuse, the resource can be equally wasted through underuse in an anti-commons. Too many owners creates potential of their blocking each other. Let's start with the commons: intuition and parallels and contradictions. Taping just a few weeks after Elinor Ostrom won the Nobel Prize; work talked about in book. What are some of the ways that societies deal with common property to avoid the tragedy? Privatization not the only one. Historically, when people thought about the commons they often thought about what is now called more precisely "open access"--that absolutely anyone can use a resource. Hard to have conservation outcomes in an open-access regime. What privatization does is align individual incentives with the resource. Historically two other solutions noted for the tragedy of the commons. One: state control. Command and control, regulation: state tells you how much you can do. Privatization often seen as a solution to over-regulation in a commons. Ostrom, basis for Nobel Prize: noticing the conditions under which commons property can succeed outside of state control on the one hand and privatization on the other. Circumstances that groups can manage resources effectively even though they are commons to the insiders and private to the outsiders. Ostrom noticed aspects like repeat play--having relationships that go across the resource and other resources; having a small number of people; sharing the same church. She's identified the circumstances in which commons resources can perform economically at a very high level.
4:54	In those situations, norms or informal rules emerge through the cooperation of those players; and they are enforced not by the state but by repeat play or connections between people that would create affection or some sort of altruistic play between them. Altruistic on the one hand, but also wealth-maximizing on the other. Don't need to look to the leviathan or complete atomization--intermediate solution. Altruism: if I personally benefit and impose costs on the rest of the group, if I care about the rest of the group that tendency will be mitigated somewhat. What Ostrom showed was this whole range of possibilities between altruism and wealth-maximization between what has long been thought to be the entire spectrum of ownership. In the middle are some interesting institutions. Anticommons idea and what gridlock book suggests is that there is an entire half of property relations and a lot of economic problems on the other side of private property. Commons-to-private is only half the spectrum; the other half spans from private to anti-commons ownership. So, in the anti-commons--how would that work? The commons is familiar. "Overuse" has been a word in English for 400 years. The problem of underuse--of resources being destroyed by being wasted by not being deployed is almost completely invisible. "Underuse" only became a word in English--in Scrabble--three or four years ago. One-way ratchet. Many examples where the real economic problem is that there are multiple owners competing with each other, resource left economically idle. What is the most underused natural resource in America? Airwaves. Artifact of the way we define property rights in broadcast spectrum. We have thousands of owners with non-transferable limited use; virtually impossible to assemble national high-speed wireless network. Because so many fragmented owners, United States has fallen out of the top ten, almost out of the top twenty. Missing wireless and entertainment services that are available in the rest of the world. Tom Hazlett: tragedy of the telecommons, cost the U.S. economy trillions of dollars.
9:38	Hard to take advantage of the potential of the system because of what we would call transactions costs--hard to negotiate with so many owners. One way to understand a tragedy of the anticommons, like a tragedy of the commons, is that these are both different versions of transactions costs failures. In the anticommons, in theory you could purchase all those rights and have control. In open-access story, harder to do. Everyone could agree not to fish. Perfectly symmetrical. Buchanan and Yoon paper: mathematical symmetry. Wireless life pleasant: what would a visitor from overseas notice that is missing? Possibility for a lot more remote medical work, easy and powerful video conferencing; real time multimedia; downloads watching TV in real time on cell phone; shopping beaming advertising and coupons to phone. Nextel network. Hard to know you are missing something if you only look within the United States. Podcast with Hazlett. What is the policy solution that would unlock those resources and get rid of the gridlock? First step is to notice that there is an economic loss from the misspecification of property rights. How we define property rights is more powerful than people realize. We don't know to be upset with the allocation of spectrum licenses because we don't know what we are missing. FCC policy. What can they do? Challenge is how to assemble resources in a low-transactions cost way. Wider array of uses and easier transferability. Make telecom licenses look more like ordinary property. Not so much fragmentation and number of owners as it is the nontransferability and restrictions on use.
14:23	Use of patents: medicine and music. Hidden example of gridlock. A lot of cutting edge treatments, gene therapy, require intervention across a whole range of genes, and each of those genes is separately patented. Diagnostic tool for cancer. Imagine walking into an auditorium and it's filled with all the owners of all the relevant patents. Unless you can get agreement from everyone in the room, the tool or therapy doesn't work. Each of those is usually some small biotech startup and each is usually very committed to the value of their individual patent and each demand the corresponding price. Hold-up problem; complementary input--need all the sections of train track to get from here to there, and if you miss any one of them you get nothing. Separate ownership is sub-optimal. In the drug context, as drug development has moved from a single patent, single molecule, innovation is increasingly breaking down. In the last year, 40,000 gene patents granted; steady increase in drug R&D investment. Number of drugs that treat disease has been going steadily down. Drug discovery gap, due to many problems such as FDA approval; but an important part is patent gridlock. Drugs that could and should exist that aren't being created. Patent system is suppose to spur innovation; paradoxically, too many patents can mean fewer life-saving innovations. Golden rice: tension between over and under patenting. If you want to have some high tech innovation in the world of plants, it use to be the case that you hybridized traits you wanted. Produced the green revolution. Inputs used to all be in the public domain, just like drug discovery. More and more those inputs are being separately patented. Freedom to create new plant products is more and more hedged in by the patents that you confront. Scientists Potrykus and Beyer: child blindness stems from Vitamin A deficiency around the world. Simple way to solve that would be to have the Vitamin A produced by the ordinary rice that is eaten. Figured out a way to do this, but in the process of engineering that new golden rice, they discovered that it infringed on roughly 70 U.S. patents. Rice is ready to go and could save millions of lives, but couldn't bring it to market because to do so they would have to negotiate dozens of separate patents. Already had this valuable product in hand; were able to shame the patent owners to create a humanitarian license. But for similar examples are ones we don't even hear about. Anger that these property rights were preventing him from getting the product to market, but realized that these patents had created the techniques that allowed him ultimately to create his product. Tension there. Fundamental policy question. Some examples in book result of accidents; some result of policy decisions made in the past or private decisions where it's hard to anticipate problems would come down the road. Is it a mistake to allow people to patent a particular gene? Is suggestion that we should have less? zero? What about products that are very expensive to bring to market because of the cost of assembling property rights? Want to extend the debate to include the possibility of hidden cost. Patents are a good thing. If it weren't for patents, there wouldn't be the biotech revolution. Massive inflow of money into biotech research arises because it's possible to get production. What gridlock discussion suggests is that against that general backdrop of patents being a good thing, we need to think about what are the potential costs. Privatization isn't the endpoint--it's an optimum between overuse and underuse, between commons and anticommons. What we are aiming for is not the most amount of property rights, but the best design for property rights. A lot of implications for that insight for patent law. Past week conference on the gridlock economy, podcast online. Particularly salient for semiconductors and telecomm, banking, internet shopping--require assembly of thousands of patents. Several thousand patents involved in a cell phone; no way to do online banking without dealing with ten thousand patents. Impossible to discover the patents you need, and impossible to know whether the claims cover your product or not. Cannot design high tech product that isn't infringing on hundreds of patents. Products that are most successful are subject to hold up by patent owners who can claim willful infringement even if you didn't even know their patent was involved.
25:00	Anticipating that legal cost, a lot of people are uninterested in innovation. Tragedy of anticommons. With a tragedy of the commons, very visible--you can see the pollution in the air or that you are not catching the same number of fish in the ocean. Tragedy of the anticommons tends to be invisible. It's the drugs that aren't invented, cellphones that don't come to market. Invisible tragedy. Michele Boldrin podcast: getting rid of patents. Legal scholars and economists on how to treat intellectual property relative to past treatment. Where are we going? We should be paying more attention to the gridlock features of patents. Mike Meurer , Jim Bessen: Patent Failure. Tried to measure overall effect of patents on economic growth. Leaving aside pharmaceuticals and chemicals, where patents seem to be wealth-producing, the net is that patents are wealth-destroying. Googles of the world would rather live in a world where property is not protected through patents but through other means. Encouraging if true: patent environment is a sort of commons; if all players think we are degrading it then we will probably move toward a different environment down the road. Talk to staffers on the Hill: how do we get patent reform? Bill aimed at patent gridlock; Congress won't go forward without bipartisan agreement, where bipartisan is not Democrat and Republican but pharmaceuticals and Information Technology (IT). True of any common solution--existing set of owners, have to find a way to compensate them if you need their support for a transition. Patent law written in 1952 to support a style of innovation, not the way America innovates any more. Compromise reached fifty years ago. Patent law is not tuned to the new style of innovation.
30:52	Music and documentaries: mashup or bundling of the nature of innovation. In the world of patent, one-patent, one-product. In the world of artistic expression--music or film or TV--today much less trying to put together separate pieces. Eyes on the Prize, documentary on Dr. Martin Luther King put together 20 years ago, Henry Hampton. Hampton pulled video clips from 80 archives; had 300 photos; about 120 fonts; won an Emmy, but disappeared. Couldn't be burned on a DVD, couldn't be shown on TV, because he didn't control all those small pieces of copyrights he needed. Most important film account of the American civil rights movement couldn't be seen. No central registry of copyright owners; even if you can identify them, have to bargain with each of them; can't assemble all the licenses. Clearing rights in the copyright world: half-Sherlock Holmes, half-Monty Hall. Many products don't come to market because even a second can become very costly. Nature of rap music has changed because of this gridlock; if you are a fan of old hip hop, Chuck D, Public Enemy, used to rap over a collage of sound. Rappers today don't do that--instead use single sample because licensing 300 is impossible. Whole style of expression is illegal. Also true for film and television. Would think there would be an exemption for fair use; but publishers and music companies are uneasy about testing that limit. In Russ's last book, wanted to use a quote of about 3 words from a movie; publisher, Princeton U. Press, insisted on permission; negotiation with multiple sources. Copyright in American law is never an absolute right. Want to give authors some protection in order to promote the writing of books. Don't want to give too much or in cases where it will reduce speech. Exemptions where permission isn't required--fair use, built into copyright laws going back to the 1700s. Previously unnoticed virtue of the fair use exemption is that it becomes a place where you can solve gridlock. Wider that exception is, the easier to assemble a collage of small snippets. University presses often on the wrong side of this debate, in favor of strong copyright and limited fair use. Usually professors want the widest dissemination of their work; often willing to give it away for free. Payment to faculty is more in prestige and recognition. Fair use policies are not decided by faculty, but by universities' general counsel office; worried about potential costs of being sued. Reasonable use--what people are actually doing. Trying to brush you back from the plate, like baseball; changes the strike zone. Only way to get right to use that 35 words is to assert it. The person who wrote those 35 words wouldn't mind seeing those words in the book. K-Pax, Kevin Spacey movie--not hurting their sales. Got the rights, easy once right person to talk to was found; relatively cheap. Eye on the Prize--also got the rights. Part of the reason in both cases that that happened is that it had a little of the flavor of the golden rice story--can cajole people when the cause is noble and desirable. Want to design a property rights system that creates a lot of wealth. Gridlock book written at non-technical level, lots of photos--each had to be licensed. Deed to a square inch of land in Alaska from Quaker Oats cereal in boxes in the late 1950s. What if you needed to assemble that land for some purpose? Picture took weeks to track down; worked out a quitclaim agreement. University press books look so boring because most people don't have the patience and curiosity to do all these negotiations.
42:25	One of the costs of this isn't just the back and forth of negotiation--long time listeners, right to use the opening music for EconTalk was given. First choice was the opening bars of If I Had a Million Dollars, by Bare Naked Ladies; had a lot of trouble trying to get the publisher to agree; wanted a very high price. Would like to think that the men of Bare Naked Ladies would be proud to give us the rights. But they don't control those rights. World Copyright Summit. Piracy a big concern. Biggest concern is not piracy any more but gridlock. So difficult to assemble the rights people need that people end up either not using them or being pirates. Piracy is an artifact of breaking through gridlock. Fragmented rights. No central registry: wouldn't that help? There is no central clearing house. Would that be an enterprise worth pushing? Costs and who bears them? We have a registry for land; can pretty much figure out who controls land. Cars--we have the DMV. For copyright, have an enormous number of works and an enormous number of rights in them, which fragments quickly over time as the original creator dies. In music context--lyrics, performer, all different kinds of rights which fragment. Google Books trying to address this problem of fragmented ownership by scanning all the world's books, central pile, jump start a registry of books. Right now they have digitized many works that are in the public domain. Lots of digitized versions of books written before 1923. There are books that are in copyright and in print: publishers control them, can negotiate. But there are roughly 20 million books that are under copyright but not in print. Can't go to publisher and buy them. Bulk of knowledge from 20th century: unless you live near a library no way to access them or search it easily. Google is scanning those books and trying to make them available at the word level--can search by word; if you want to download it, you pay a fee, which goes to the central registry. How can Google do that if they don't own the rights to the books? Suit between authors' guild and Google, which claims what they are doing is fair use. Since they are out of print, the owners aren't making money off them anyway. Can discover it, quote it. Hasn't been settled. Negotiated settlement attracted opposition over the last few months; other countries have objected, anti-trust; settlement pending. Trying to solve a basic gridlock problem. Class action mechanism, aggregates a series of claims no one of which is worth pursuing individually but collectively generates a lot of value.
52:11	Two issues: Example of air space. How did that evolve--flying over your house: could you stop an airplane for trespassing? Intuition: When you own a piece of land you own up to the heavens and down the center of the earth, a column of ownership. Makes sense for 50 feet up, but how about 1000 feet up? Not an issue till the invention of airplanes in the 20th century; no one had ever thought about it before. Overfly from NYC to DC means flying over the property of thousands of people, each one of whom would potentially have a claim against you. Lawyers and policy makers struggled how to make it possible. Part of solution was to say that you own up to the heavens, but as a matter of law, the heavens is 1000 feet up. Solved the gridlock problem and made it possible to fly by a timely redefinition. Airports: hard to build an airport. Public policy solution sometimes invokes eminent domain. A lot of private solutions also work--don't make a public announcement, buy land privately. Public solution feasible for Martin Luther King documentary--could pass a law saying this documentary that showed on TV with all of its citations and visuals, the documentary maker is entitled to them all; same way as with for an airport or shopping mall. Is that likely? Has it been abused? Public choice problems. People hate having their homes taken by eminent domain. You can always solve the problem by eliminating private ownership. Last-best solution in every case. Kelo. In intellectual property rights, in drug area, there already exists a "march in" right--the government already has the right to march in and take patents. If ever exercised, would crush the incentive of drug firms to spend a billion dollars to create a new drug and get it through the FDA approval process. First and most important step is to note that there is a problem. Then think about tools for making it easier for entrepreneurs in the market to assemble rights. Radio play; patent pools: you can pop a DVD disk into a DVD player is the several hundred patents required to make that work have been pooled together and you play one licensing fee to the pool together. What kinds of market institutions might be able to facilitate?
Listen to the  above podcast transcript with Russ Roberts and Michael Heller on gridlock and the tragedy of
the anticommons (posted to Sakai or available through econtalk.org) up to the 34:06
mark1, and respond to the following prompts. Your responses do not need to form a
cohesive essay. Instead focus on concisely responding to each prompt. The last page of this
document provides updates to issues brought up during the 2009 podcast: Authors Guild
Inc. vs. Google Inc., gene patenting, and electromagnetic spectrum allocation. 1. In 2 to 4 sentences, describe what the term “gridlock” or “tragedy of the
anticommons” refers to.

The term "gridlock" or "tragedy of the anticommons" refers to a situation in which a resource is underused or wasted, not because of overuse as in the tragedy of the commons, but due to too many individuals or entities holding rights to that resource. This excessive fragmentation of ownership creates barriers to cooperation and effective utilization, as the numerous owners can block or prevent each other from making the most productive use of the resource. As a result, the value that could be generated from the resource for the broader society is lost, leading to economic inefficiencies and missed opportunities for wealth creation.
Ostrom, basis for Nobel Prize: noticing the conditions under which commons property can succeed outside of state control on the one hand and privatization on the other.  what does this mean?

Elinor Ostrom, who was awarded the Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences in 2009, is renowned for her pioneering research on the governance of common-pool resources—those that are shared among communities such as fisheries, pastures, and forests. Her work critically expanded the understanding of how common property can be managed sustainably and productively outside the traditional dichotomy of state control and privatization.

Ostrom's Nobel Prize-winning insights lie in her identification of the conditions under which commons management succeeds without the need for either heavy-handed government intervention (state control) or complete privatization, where resources are owned and managed by individuals or corporations. She found that communities around the world have developed a variety of institutional arrangements for managing common-pool resources in ways that avoid both overuse (the "tragedy of the commons") and underuse (the "tragedy of the anticommons").

These conditions include:

1. **Clearly defined boundaries:** Identifying who has rights to use the resource and what the resource is.
2. **Congruence between appropriation and provision rules and local conditions:** Making sure that the rules for using the resource and contributing to its maintenance are adapted to local needs and conditions.
3. **Collective-choice arrangements:** Ensuring that those affected by the rules can participate in modifying the rules.
4. **Effective monitoring:** Making sure that monitors, who are either resource users or accountable to the users, keep an eye on the resource.
5. **Graduated sanctions:** Implementing a system of sanctions for rule violators that starts off lenient but becomes stricter.
6. **Conflict-resolution mechanisms:** Having accessible and low-cost ways to resolve disputes among users or between users and managers.
7. **Minimal recognition of rights to organize:** Ensuring that the government accepts the right of users to create their own institutions and does not undermine them.
8. **Nested enterprises:** For larger common-pool resources, organizing governance activities in multiple layers of nested enterprises.

Through her research, Ostrom disproved the conventional wisdom that common resources inevitably lead to ruin due to individuals acting out of self-interest, showing instead that communal governance, under the right conditions, can lead to sustainable and equitable resource management. This contribution was groundbreaking because it showcased successful examples of commons management and outlined specific factors that can enable communities to sustainably manage shared resources outside of privatization or state control, adding a nuanced third option to the conventional binary choice.
what are the solutions he suggested for anti-commons

Michael Heller, who coined the term "tragedy of the anticommons," suggests several solutions to address and mitigate the negative impacts of this phenomenon. These solutions aim to minimize the underuse of resources that can occur when too many owners hold veto power over the use of a shared asset, making it difficult to aggregate those rights into a single hand for effective and efficient utilization. While Heller discusses these solutions across various contexts, including real estate, intellectual property, and spectrum rights, the underlying principles can be broadly applied:

1. **Streamlining Property Rights:** Simplifying the structure of property rights to reduce the number of owners with a claim on a single resource can help mitigate gridlock. This could involve regulatory or legal changes that make it easier to consolidate ownership or use rights, thereby reducing the barriers to effective resource utilization.

2. **Creating Clearer Legal Frameworks:** Developing more defined legal frameworks that outline how resources can be shared and used can help reduce the uncertainty that often leads to gridlock. Clarifying the rules around property rights and usage can facilitate negotiations and agreements among multiple stakeholders.

3. **Facilitating Negotiations:** Establishing platforms, institutions, or services that help owners negotiate the use of shared resources can overcome coordination problems. These might include setting up registries, exchanges, or collective management organizations that can act on behalf of multiple owners to grant usage rights to third parties.

4. **Reducing Transaction Costs:** Transaction costs are a major barrier to efficient resource use in situations of fragmented ownership. Reducing these costs through legal reforms, technological solutions, or market mechanisms can make it easier for interested parties to negotiate and reach agreements.

5. **Encouraging Patent Pools and Licensing Agreements:** In the context of intellectual property, encouraging the formation of patent pools, where multiple patent holders combine their patents into a single portfolio and make them available for licensing, can reduce the anticommons effect. Similarly, compulsory licensing or broader licensing agreements can ensure that patents do not obstruct innovation and product development.

6. **Eminent Domain and Compulsory Purchase:** In extreme cases, government intervention through eminent domain or compulsory purchase orders can resolve gridlock by forcibly consolidating ownership in the public interest. However, this approach is controversial and comes with its own set of ethical and practical challenges.

7. **Improving Public Policy and Regulatory Approaches:** Policymakers can play a role in preventing anticommons scenarios by carefully considering the implications of new regulations, particularly in areas like zoning, intellectual property, and spectrum allocation. Crafting policies that anticipate and mitigate fragmentation can prevent gridlock before it occurs.

8. **Exploring Cooperative Solutions:** Encouraging cooperative approaches to resource management, where owners form collectives or associations to manage a resource jointly, can also provide a solution to the anticommons tragedy. This approach draws on Elinor Ostrom's work on managing commons and can be adapted to prevent the underuse associated with fragmented ownership.

These solutions require carefully balanced approaches that consider the specific context and the nature of the resource being managed. The goal is to find a middle ground that enables efficient use of resources without tilting too far towards either excessive privatization or fragmentation.

2. In 2 to 4 sentences, describe how the tragedy of the anticommons is related to the
tragedy of the commons. In the podcast, they are described as being on opposite
ends of a spectrum. What spectrum are they referring to (i.e., what is the spectrum
over)?

The tragedy of the anticommons and the tragedy of the commons are two sides of a spectrum regarding the management and utilization of resources, with this spectrum being over the degree of fragmentation in ownership rights. In the tragedy of the commons, the problem arises from insufficient ownership or overly shared access, leading to overuse and degradation of a resource because no single entity holds exclusive rights to limit its use. Conversely, the tragedy of the anticommons occurs when there are too many owners, each with the right to exclude others from a resource, which leads to underuse because the coordination among numerous owners becomes too cumbersome or expensive, preventing the resource's effective utilization. Thus, the spectrum ranges from open, unrestricted access and use at one end (commons) to excessive fragmentation of exclusive rights at the other (anticommons), illustrating different challenges in resource management based on ownership structures.
3. In one paragraph, describe how the tragedy of the anticommons manifests itself in creative expression markets (e.g., writing, music).

The tragedy of the anticommons significantly impacts creative expression markets, such as writing, music, and filmmaking, by creating a fragmented landscape of intellectual property rights that can stifle innovation and artistic expression. In these markets, a piece of creative work often incorporates or builds upon existing works, necessitating permission from and potentially payments to multiple rights holders. For instance, a musician looking to sample various songs to create a new track might find the process prohibitively expensive and legally complex due to the need to negotiate with every rights holder of the original tracks. Similarly, filmmakers and authors may encounter significant barriers when trying to include copyrighted materials (such as music, photos, quotes, or film clips) in their works, as each piece of copyrighted material can be owned by different entities, each with the power to veto its use. This fragmentation can lead to creative projects being significantly altered, delayed, or abandoned due to the high transaction costs and legal risks associated with securing all necessary permissions. The result is a chilling effect on creative innovation, where the fear of potential litigation or the burden of navigating the complex web of intellectual property rights can deter artists from pursuing new projects or exploring innovative ideas, ultimately limiting the diversity and richness of cultural expression available to society.

4. In one to two paragraphs, propose a solution to the problem you identified in #3.
Concisely describe the costs and benefits of the proposed solution.

A viable solution to mitigate the tragedy of the anticommons in creative markets is the implementation and expansion of a fair use doctrine, complemented by the establishment of a centralized licensing clearinghouse. The fair use doctrine allows creators to use copyrighted material without obtaining permission under certain conditions, such as for commentary, criticism, news reporting, or research, thus providing a legal framework that supports innovation and creativity by balancing the interests of original copyright holders with the broader public interest in promoting cultural and artistic expression. Expanding the scope of fair use could lower legal barriers for creators, making it easier to work with existing material in new, transformative ways that contribute to culture and knowledge without undermining the economic rights of original creators.

In tandem, establishing a centralized licensing clearinghouse would streamline the process of acquiring permissions and negotiating fees for copyrighted material, reducing transaction costs and legal uncertainties for creators. This clearinghouse could act as a one-stop shop for rights clearances, offering standardized rates and simplifying the process of rights acquisition across multiple jurisdictions and types of media. For copyright holders, this system would ensure a more consistent and efficient mechanism for monetizing and protecting their intellectual property, while for creators, it would lower the barriers to accessing and incorporating existing works into new creations.

The benefits of this dual approach include fostering a more vibrant and dynamic creative ecosystem by reducing legal hurdles and financial burdens on creators, potentially leading to an increase in the production and diversity of cultural content available to the public. The fair use expansion provides a safety net for creators to experiment and innovate, while the centralized licensing model offers a practical solution for managing copyright permissions more efficiently.

However, the costs may involve the complexities of implementing and enforcing a broader fair use policy that respects the rights of copyright holders while promoting creative freedom, as well as the potential challenges in establishing and maintaining a centralized licensing system that is fair, efficient, and responsive to the needs of both creators and copyright holders. There could also be concerns from copyright owners about potential revenue loss or misuse of their materials under an expanded fair use regime, necessitating a careful balance between encouraging creative innovation and ensuring fair compensation for original creators.
In one to two paragraphs,
It seems that your question was cut off before you could finish typing it. Could you please provide more details or complete the question you'd like to ask?

4. In one to two paragraphs, propose a solution to the problem you identified in #3.
Concisely describe the costs and benefits of the proposed solution.

A proactive solution to the tragedy of the anticommons in creative markets involves the establishment of a collective rights organization (CRO) tailored specifically for creative industries such as music, film, and literature. This organization would function as a clearinghouse for managing copyrights and facilitating the licensing process. By aggregating the rights of multiple copyright holders into a singular, manageable entity, the CRO would significantly streamline the process of obtaining permission for using copyrighted material. Creators wishing to incorporate existing works into their new creations could negotiate with a single entity rather than navigating the labyrinth of individual copyright holders. Additionally, the CRO could establish standardized licensing fees and terms, reducing transaction costs and legal uncertainty for creative professionals. This approach could also include the development of a comprehensive digital database that provides transparent information on copyright ownership and licensing requirements, further easing the burden on creators looking to use copyrighted material.

The primary benefit of this solution is the reduction of barriers to creativity and innovation within critical cultural industries. By simplifying the process of rights clearance, creators can more freely engage in the kind of sampling, adaptation, and transformative work that drives artistic evolution and cultural enrichment. Moreover, copyright holders would benefit from a more efficient mechanism to monetize their works, potentially leading to increased revenues and greater incentive for creative production. On the cost side, this solution necessitates significant initial investment to establish and maintain the CRO, and there would be ongoing operational expenses. There's also the challenge of ensuring fair representation and compensation for all copyright holders, including those with smaller or less commercially significant works. Additionally, this system relies on widespread participation by copyright holders to be truly effective, necessitating buy-in from across the creative sector. Despite these challenges, the potential to unlock creative collaboration and innovation makes the investment into such a system a worthwhile endeavor for the future of creative expression.

A viable solution to mitigate the tragedy of the anticommons in creative markets is the implementation and expansion of a fair use doctrine, complemented by the establishment of a centralized licensing clearinghouse. The fair use doctrine allows creators to use copyrighted material without obtaining permission under certain conditions, such as for commentary, criticism, news reporting, or research, thus providing a legal framework that supports innovation and creativity by balancing the interests of original copyright holders with the broader public interest in promoting cultural and artistic expression. Expanding the scope of fair use could lower legal barriers for creators, making it easier to work with existing material in new, transformative ways that contribute to culture and knowledge without undermining the economic rights of original creators.

In tandem, establishing a centralized licensing clearinghouse would streamline the process of acquiring permissions and negotiating fees for copyrighted material, reducing transaction costs and legal uncertainties for creators. This clearinghouse could act as a one-stop shop for rights clearances, offering standardized rates and simplifying the process of rights acquisition across multiple jurisdictions and types of media. For copyright holders, this system would ensure a more consistent and efficient mechanism for monetizing and protecting their intellectual property, while for creators, it would lower the barriers to accessing and incorporating existing works into new creations.

The benefits of this dual approach include fostering a more vibrant and dynamic creative ecosystem by reducing legal hurdles and financial burdens on creators, potentially leading to an increase in the production and diversity of cultural content available to the public. The fair use expansion provides a safety net for creators to experiment and innovate, while the centralized licensing model offers a practical solution for managing copyright permissions more efficiently.

However, the costs may involve the complexities of implementing and enforcing a broader fair use policy that respects the rights of copyright holders while promoting creative freedom, as well as the potential challenges in establishing and maintaining a centralized licensing system that is fair, efficient, and responsive to the needs of both creators and copyright holders. There could also be concerns from copyright owners about potential revenue loss or misuse of their materials under an expanded fair use regime, necessitating a careful balance between encouraging creative innovation and ensuring fair compensation for original creators.   Please concise and shorten to one to two paragaphs

To address the tragedy of the anticommons in creative markets, expanding the fair use doctrine alongside establishing a centralized licensing clearinghouse presents a viable solution. By broadening the scope of fair use, creators gain legal flexibility to engage with existing copyrighted materials for purposes like commentary, criticism, and research without dire legal repercussions, fostering an environment that encourages innovation and cultural contribution. A centralized licensing clearinghouse would further alleviate the issue by acting as a one-stop shop for negotiating and acquiring permissions, offering standardized rates and reducing the transactional friction associated with using copyrighted works. This dual strategy would not only invigorate creativity and diversity in cultural content but also provide a more streamlined and equitable way for copyright holders to monetize their intellectual property.

However, this approach comes with its set of challenges, including the complexity of broadening fair use in a manner that balances creators' freedoms with copyright holders' rights, as well as establishing and maintaining a centralized licensing entity that is efficient, fair, and universally acceptable. There's a potential concern about revenue impact on copyright holders and the overarching need for a system that ensures fair compensation. Despite these hurdles, the combined benefits of fostering a more robust creative environment and reducing legal barriers for creators make pursuing these solutions critical for the evolution of creative markets.

5. In 2 to 4 sentences, explain how class-action lawsuits are a solution to the tragedy of
the anticommons.

Class-action lawsuits can offer a solution to the tragedy of the anticommons by aggregating individual legal claims into a collective action, thereby overcoming the fragmentation and coordination problems that can stymie the use of shared resources. This approach allows a group of affected individuals or entities, who might otherwise face prohibitive costs and logistical challenges in asserting their rights individually, to pool their resources and legal standing. By consolidating numerous similar claims into a single, coherent legal proceeding, class-action lawsuits can efficiently address and resolve the gridlock situations that the anticommons creates, potentially leading to reforms or compensation that facilitate better access to and utilization of the contested resource.

6. The Great Recession was due in large part to the collapse of the housing market
from 2007-2009. As a brief background on the collapse, financial assets called
mortgage backed securities became more widely used leading up the crisis. These
securities bundle together claims to the interest earned on mortgages and can be
bought and sold like any other financial asset (e.g. stocks or bonds). As these assets
became more widely used, banks had a stronger incentive to provide mortgages to
subprime (risky) borrowers because mortgage backed securities allowed them to
essentially sell off the risk of individuals defaulting (foreclosing) on their mortgage
to outside investors.2 This is widely viewed as the primary reason for the housing
collapse, but it was exacerbated by the following problem. Traditionally when an
individual is at risk of defaulting on a mortgage, the bank (the lender) will work to
restructure the loan because default is costly for all parties involved. However, in the case of mortgage backed securities, many individuals now had claims to a
portion of the value of the assets that could be recovered in the event of a default
(i.e., there’s been a movement away a one lender and one borrower relationship to a
multiple lenders and one borrower relationship). Explain how the increased
sharing of these rights is related to the tragedy of the anticommons and led to an
increase in the number of defaults (relative to the traditional case of one lender and
one borrower).

The scenario outlined with the collapse of the housing market and the role of mortgage-backed securities (MBS) exemplifies a financial anticommons situation, where the fragmentation of ownership rights—in this case, claims on mortgage interest payments—contributed to an increase in defaults. In traditional mortgage arrangements, when a borrower was at risk of defaulting, there was a direct relationship between the borrower and the lender (usually a bank), enabling them to negotiate terms, restructure the loan, or find other solutions to avoid foreclosure. However, with the advent of MBS, the rights to the mortgage payments were split among numerous investors, spreading the risk but also complicating the decision-making process.

This "multiple lenders to one borrower" relationship complicated efforts to restructure loans for distressed borrowers. Each of these investors held a piece of the rights to the mortgage payments, but no single party had a complete stake or the authority to initiate a renegotiation of the loan terms without seeking consensus from a potentially vast and dispersed group of security holders. This fragmentation created a scenario akin to the tragedy of the anticommons, where the excessive division of rights and the lack of a centralized decision-making authority led to underuse of the resource—in this case, the potential for productive loan restructuring. The gridlock created by the divided ownership in mortgage assets made timely and effective interventions to prevent defaults much more difficult, thus exacerbating the foreclosure crisis. The barriers to cooperation among the multitude of MBS investors resulted in higher foreclosure rates than might have occurred under traditional lending models, highlighting a clear linkage between the fragmentation of financial claims and adverse outcomes in the housing market.
concise

The increased fragmentation of mortgage rights through mortgage-backed securities (MBS) is akin to the tragedy of the anticommons, where the division of ownership complicates decisions regarding asset use—in this case, loan restructuring to prevent defaults. With MBS, the borrower's loan is tied to multiple investors, eliminating the straightforward lender-borrower relationship and hindering negotiation processes. This dispersed ownership made it challenging to unify investors' interests for proactive solutions, leading to a higher incidence of defaults. Essentially, the complexity in ownership diluted the ability to respond effectively to at-risk mortgages, exemplifying how the tragedy of the anticommons exacerbated the housing market collapse.

However, with the advent of MBS, the rights to the mortgage payments were split among numerous investors, spreading the risk but also complicating the decision-making process.

This “multiple lenders to one borrower” relationship complicated efforts to restructure loans for distressed borrowers. Each of these investors held a piece of the rights to the mortgage payments, but no single party had a complete stake or the authority to initiate a renegotiation of the loan terms without seeking consensus from a potentially vast and dispersed group of security holders. The collapse of the housing market, intensified by the use of mortgage-backed securities (MBS), serves as a financial example of the tragedy of the anticommons. MBS fragmented the ownership of mortgage rights among many investors, disrupting the direct lender-borrower relationship and complicating loan restructuring efforts for at-risk borrowers. The division of these rights meant no single investor could easily initiate renegotiations, creating a decision-making gridlock akin to the anticommons, where the potential for productive outcomes—such as loan restructuring—was underused. This fragmentation and lack of centralized authority to address distress in loans contributed to a rise in defaults, demonstrating how fragmented financial claims can lead to detrimental outcomes in the housing market.

7. List 3 things that were either unclear about the podcast or readings, or general
questions related to the material that you would like to know more about.

Given that I can't listen to podcasts or read external materials, here are three hypothetical questions or areas of potential confusion that could arise from a discussion on the tragedy of the anticommons, based on typical complexities surrounding the topic:

1. **Mechanisms of Resolution:** How are situations of anticommons, particularly in complex systems like financial markets or intellectual property, practically resolved? It would be informative to understand specific instances or case studies where solutions have successfully mitigated the effects of the anticommons, especially concerning patent pools or collective rights management in the creative industries.

2. **Broader Economic Impact:** What is the broader economic impact of the tragedy of the anticommons, beyond specific sectors like real estate or technology? For example, how does the fragmentation of ownership rights affect national or global economic growth and innovation, and are there quantifiable metrics to measure this impact?

3. **Policy Interventions and Legal Frameworks:** What specific policy interventions, legal frameworks, or economic mechanisms have been proposed or implemented to prevent or alleviate the tragedy of the anticommons? Detailed explanations of how different jurisdictions or sectors have approached the challenge of fragmented ownership rights, and the efficacy of these measures, would provide deeper insights into potential solutions to the problem.

7. List 3 things that were either unclear about the podcast transcript or readings, or general
questions related to the material that you would like to know more about.

Since I cannot directly access or listen to specific podcasts, transcripts, or readings, I'll formulate three general questions or areas where listeners or readers might seek further clarity or additional information from a discussion on the tragedy of the anticommons and related topics:

1. **The Role of Regulatory Bodies:** How do regulatory bodies, like the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) in the context of spectrum rights or intellectual property offices for patents, navigate the balance between preventing the tragedy of the anticommons and encouraging innovation? Understanding the mechanisms through which these entities influence the distribution and fragmentation of rights would provide insight into potential levers for mitigating the anticommons phenomenon.

2. **International Comparisons and Solutions:** Are there examples from other countries or international systems that have effectively managed the risks associated with the tragedy of the anticommons, particularly in areas like intellectual property, real estate development, or natural resources? Exploring successful international practices could illuminate alternative approaches to managing fragmented ownership and its consequences.

3. **Long-term Effects on Innovation:** What are the long-term implications of the tragedy of the anticommons for innovation, especially in fast-evolving sectors like biotechnology, software development, and digital content creation? In-depth discussion on how fragmented ownership rights might stifle innovation or alter the trajectory of technological advancement could highlight the importance of addressing the anticommons in shaping the future of economies and societies.

Explain. Gridlock–too many owners–riff on familiar concept of tragedy of the commons. In a tragedy of the commons, too few owners, resulting in overuse of the resource. Too few owners in the ocean and people over-fish it; too few owners of the air, and people pollute it. Solution to tragedy of the commons is to privatize–single decision-maker who invests in the lake so there are more fish tomorrow. But privatization can overshoot. Instead of too few owners and a tragedy of the commons, we can have too many owners, and a tragedy of the anticommons. Instead of a resource being wasted in the economic sense through overuse, the resource can be equally wasted through underuse in an anti-commons. Too many owners creates potential of their blocking each other. Let’s start with the commons: intuition and parallels and contradictions. Taping just a few weeks after Elinor Ostrom won the Nobel Prize; work talked about in book. What are some of the ways that societies deal with common property to avoid the tragedy? Privatization not the only one. Historically, when people thought about the commons they often thought about what is now called more precisely “open access”–that absolutely anyone can use a resource. Hard to have conservation outcomes in an open-access regime. What privatization does is align individual incentives with the resource. Historically two other solutions noted for the tragedy of the commons. One: state control. Command and control, regulation: state tells you how much you can do. The tragedy of the anticommons significantly impacts creative expression markets, such as writing, music, and filmmaking, by creating a fragmented landscape of intellectual property rights that can stifle innovation and artistic expression. In these markets, a piece of creative work often incorporates or builds upon existing works, necessitating permission from and potentially payments to multiple rights holders. For instance, a musician looking to sample various songs to create a new track might find the process prohibitively expensive and legally complex due to the need to negotiate with every rights holder of the original tracks. Similarly, filmmakers and authors may encounter significant barriers when trying to include copyrighted materials (such as music, photos, quotes, or film clips) in their works, as each piece of copyrighted material can be owned by different entities, each with the power to veto its use. This fragmentation can lead to creative projects being significantly altered, delayed, or abandoned due to the high transaction costs and legal risks associated with securing all necessary permissions. The result is a chilling effect on creative innovation, where the fear of potential litigation or the burden of navigating complex intellectual property rights can deter artists from pursuing new projects or exploring innovative ideas, ultimately limiting the diversity and richness of cultural expression available to society.
shorten by a little bit

The tragedy of the anticommons hampers the creative sectors—writing, music, filmmaking—by fragmenting intellectual property rights, stifling innovation and artistic expression. Creators often need to build upon existing works, facing high costs and complex legalities in securing permissions from numerous rights holders. This can significantly delay, alter, or even halt creative projects, creating a chilling effect on innovation. The daunting prospect of potential litigation and the intricate maze of intellectual property rights discourage artists from exploring new ideas, reducing the diversity and vibrancy of cultural content available to the public.

The tragedy of the anticommons affects creative domains like music, writing, and filmmaking by creating a fragmented intellectual property landscape, which stifles innovation and artistic endeavors. In these fields, new creations often rely on existing works, necessitating permissions from multiple rights holders, which can be both costly and complex. This fragmentation leads to potential delays or cancellations of creative projects due to high transaction costs and the risk of legal challenges. Consequently, this creates a chilling effect on innovation, deterring artists from engaging with new ideas or projects. Ultimately, it limits the cultural diversity and richness available, as the complexities of navigating intellectual property rights hinder the realization of new artistic expressions.
